lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Now entertain conjecture of a time

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: When creeping murmur and the poring dark

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Fills the wide vessel of the universe.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: From camp to camp through the foul womb of night

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The hum of either army stilly sounds,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: That the fix’d sentinels almost receive

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The secret whispers of each other’s watch:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Fire answers fire, and through their paly flames

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Each battle sees the other’s umber’d face;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Steed threatens steed, in high and boastful neighs

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Piercing the night’s dull ear; and from the tents

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The armourers, accomplishing the knights,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With busy hammers closing rivets up,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Give dreadful note of preparation:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The country cocks do crow, the clocks do toll,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And the third hour of drowsy morning name.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Proud of their numbers and secure in soul,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The confident and over-lusty French

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Do the low-rated English play at dice;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And chide the cripple tardy-gaited night

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Who, like a foul and ugly witch, doth limp

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: So tediously away. The poor condemned English,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Like sacrifices, by their watchful fires

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Sit patiently and inly ruminate

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The morning’s danger, and their gesture sad

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Investing lank-lean cheeks and war-worn coats

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Presenteth them unto the gazing moon

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: So many horrid ghosts. O now, who will behold

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The royal captain of this ruin’d band

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Walking from watch to watch, from tent to tent,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Let him cry, “Praise and glory on his head!”

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: For forth he goes and visits all his host,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Bids them good morrow with a modest smile

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And calls them brothers, friends, and countrymen.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Upon his royal face there is no note

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: How dread an army hath enrounded him;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Nor doth he dedicate one jot of colour

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Unto the weary and all-watched night,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: But freshly looks and overbears attaint

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With cheerful semblance and sweet majesty;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: That every wretch, pining and pale before,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Beholding him, plucks comfort from his looks:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: A largess universal like the sun

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: His liberal eye doth give to everyone,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Thawing cold fear, that mean and gentle all

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Behold, as may unworthiness define,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: A little touch of Harry in the night.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And so our scene must to the battle fly;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Where—O for pity!—we shall much disgrace

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With four or five most vile and ragged foils,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Right ill-disposed in brawl ridiculous,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The name of Agincourt. Yet sit and see,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Minding true things by what their mock’ries be.

glosses.definition: receive a guess; imagine
quiet, spreading sound
brooding, dense darkness — 'poring' = staring intently
the whole cosmos as a vast container
night as a dark womb — productive image for the coming day's slaughter
quietly sounds
posted guards
pale, wan flames — campfires seen from a distance
shadowed, darkened face — 'umber' is a brown-earth pigment
warhorse challenges warhorse — the horses are more active than the men
armour-smiths finishing the knights' equipment — the stanza's trademark image
hammering the last rivets into armour-joints
fearsome sound of readiness for battle
excessively high-spirited French
dice for the undervalued English — they gamble for prisoners before the battle
lame, slow-walking night — the French curse its slowness
night as a hag who limps away — classic misogynist personification
like beasts prepared for sacrifice — the English pictured as victims
inwardly meditate
clothing their gaunt, thin cheeks
battle-worn uniforms
frightful spectres — the moonlit English look already dead

glosses.phrase: royal captain of this ruin’d band
glosses.definition: Henry, king of this wrecked company

glosses.phrase: Praise and glory on his head
glosses.definition: the Chorus's own benediction, overheard by the audience
surrounded him

glosses.phrase: Nor doth he dedicate one jot of colour
glosses.definition: he doesn't lose a particle of his complexion — showing no fear
overpowers (the appearance of) exhaustion — 'attaint' = infection, weariness
bounty given to all, as the sun gives light
generous gaze
low-born and high-born alike

glosses.phrase: A little touch of Harry in the night
glosses.definition: a brief passing contact with Henry in the dark — the stanza's famous phrase, and the scene's set-up for 4.1

glosses.phrase: four or five most vile and ragged foils
glosses.definition: a handful of shabby stage-swords — the Chorus's self-deprecating confession that the battle cannot be staged
the name we will honour despite the stage's failure

glosses.phrase: Minding true things by what their mock’ries be
glosses.definition: remembering true events through their theatrical imitation — the Chorus's final aesthetic plea

annotations.title: The atmospheric masterwork — night between two armies
annotations.body: The Act 4 Chorus is, for many critics, the single greatest poetic passage in Shakespeare's histories. It does not narrate events; it builds an atmosphere. The opening triplet — 'creeping murmur,' 'poring dark,' 'wide vessel of the universe' — establishes a nocturnal stillness in which the two armies are close enough to hear each other ('the fix'd sentinels almost receive / The secret whispers of each other's watch'). The visual poetry that follows — 'fire answers fire,' 'paly flames,' 'umber'd face,' 'steed threatens steed' — gives the reader a cinematic image of two camps facing each other across a dark field. The sound-world is tight: hammers closing rivets, cocks crowing, clocks tolling the third hour. The Chorus uses the stillness to set up the contrast: the 'over-lusty' French playing at dice, the 'poor condemned English' sitting like sacrifices by their watchful fires. No battle has yet begun; everything about the outcome is already ambient.
sources: Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)
Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

annotations.title: 'A little touch of Harry in the night' — the king as moral presence
annotations.body: The Chorus's portrait of Henry walking among his soldiers — 'bids them good morrow with a modest smile / And calls them brothers, friends, and countrymen' — is the play's official version of the event. Henry is generous, his liberal eye a 'largess universal like the sun'; wretches 'pluck comfort from his looks.' The tableau is hagiographic. The famous phrase 'a little touch of Harry in the night' has a tender, almost monastic cadence. But the Chorus is about to hand over to the actual 4.1 scene, which will show us Henry doing what the Chorus has described — except with a specific and conflictual encounter: the Williams debate. The Chorus and the scene do not contradict each other exactly, but the Chorus's radiant-royal generosity and the scene's soldier's critique of royal responsibility do not occupy the same moral universe. Again, both are the play; readers should hold both.
sources: Anne Barton, 'The King Disguised,' in The Triple Bond (1975)

trials.prompt: What atmosphere does the Chorus build in lines 1–16?
options: A quiet night between two armies, close enough to hear each other's sentinels — a cinematic stillness in which the hammers of armourers, the crowing of cocks, and the tolling of clocks all register

trials.prompt: What does 'A little touch of Harry in the night' (line 47) do?
options: Concludes the Chorus's hagiographic portrait of Henry as a morally radiant presence among his men — just before the 4.1 scene will complicate that portrait with Williams's challenge

trials.prompt: How does the Chorus contrast the two armies at lines 17–28?
options: The French 'over-lusty' and 'secure in soul,' playing at dice for English prisoners; the 'poor condemned English' sitting 'like sacrifices' by their fires, 'horrid ghosts' in the moonlight
The English play dice and the French pray
